title: Rice and peas
subtitle: Jamaican coconut rice with kidney beans
excerpt: Long-grain rice cooked in coconut milk and stock with kidney beans, garlic, and thyme until fluffy and fragrant. The Jamaican staple that goes with everything, particularly jerk chicken. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Rice and peas (where 'peas' means kidney beans in the Jamaican usage) is the national dish of Jamaica, served at Sunday lunch and celebrations. Documented by Enid Donaldson in The Real Taste of Jamaica (1993). The dish is also made with gungo peas (pigeon peas) in Jamaican cooking; kidney beans are the most common version in the UK.

content.text: The coconut milk replaces some of the water in the cooking liquid, giving the rice a rich, slightly sweet flavour and a creamier texture than plain rice. The thyme and allspice go in whole and are removed before serving — they are there to flavour the cooking liquid, not to eat. Do not lift the lid during cooking; the trapped steam is what gives the rice its texture.

items.prepNote: rinsed until the water runs clear
whole berries if you have them, or ground

content.text: Combine {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk with enough water to make 600 ml total cooking liquid. Put the rinsed rice in a large saucepan. Add the kidney beans, {{garlic}} crushed garlic cloves, {{thyme}} thyme sprigs, {{allspice}} of allspice, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Pour over the coconut milk mixture. Bring to the boil over medium-high heat, stir once, then reduce the heat to the very lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook for 18 minutes. Remove from the heat without lifting the lid and leave to steam for 5 minutes. Remove the thyme sprigs and garlic. Fluff with a fork and serve.

items.symptom: Rice sticking to the bottom and burning
items.cause: Heat too high during cooking, or not enough liquid.
items.fix: The lowest possible heat is essential once the lid goes on. Some sticking at the very bottom is normal and creates a crust similar to socarrat — it should be golden, not burnt.

items.cause: Too much liquid, or rice not rinsed to remove surface starch.
items.fix: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear. Use exactly 600 ml total liquid for 300 g rice.

items.cause: Lid lifted during cooking, releasing steam. Or heat too high and water evaporated before rice was cooked.
items.fix: If the rice is still firm, add a splash of water, replace the lid, and cook for 5 more minutes. Do not lift the lid again until the full time has elapsed.

content.text: Rice and peas is the Sunday dish of Jamaica — made for the midday meal, which is the main meal of the week. The word peas refers to kidney beans in Jamaican English; gungo peas (pigeon peas) are also used and are common at Christmas. The coconut milk that replaces part of the water was historically used when the preserved dried coconut was grated and soaked rather than tinned. The dish is inseparable from jerk chicken in the British-Caribbean restaurant context, though in Jamaica it accompanies stews, curried goat, and roasted meats of all kinds.
